S34: Published by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd. A B S T R A C T Background Cramps are painful, involuntary muscle contractions.
S35: They commonly aﬀect people with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis/motor neuron disease (ALS/MND) at all stages of the disease.
S36: To date, the treatment of muscle cramps in ALS has been largely empirical without any evidence from randomised controlled trials.
S37: Objectives To systematically assess the eﬀect of interventions on muscle cramps as a primary or secondary endpoint or adverse event in people with ALS/MND.
S38: Search methods We searched the Cochrane Neuromuscular Disease Group Specialized Register (14 February 2011), the Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (Issue 1, 2011 in The Cochrane Library), MEDLINE (January 1966 to January 2011) and EMBASE (January 1980 to January 2011) and reference lists of articles searched using the terms motor neuron disease, motor neurone disease, motoneuron disease or amyotrophic lateral sclerosis.
S39: We contacted authors of trials for further information.
S40: Selection criteria We included all randomised and quasi-randomised trials of oral medications in people with ALS which assessed cramps as a primary or secondary outcome measure or as an adverse event.
S41: We also included trials using subcutaneous or intravenous medications or physical therapy.
S42: Data collection and analysis All authors applied the selection criteria and assessed study quality independently, and all authors performed independent data extraction.
S43: Main results Twenty studies including 4789 participants were identified.
S44: Only one trial, of tetrahydrocannabinol (THC), assessed cramps as the primary endpoint.
S45: Thirteen studies assessed cramps as a secondary endpoint.
S46: The medications comprised vitamin E, baclofen, riluzole, L- threonine, xaliproden, indinavir, and memantine.
S47: Six studies assessed cramps as an adverse event.
S48: The medications comprised creatine, gabapentin, dextromethorphan, quinidine, and lithium.
S49: In all 20 studies no favourable eﬀect for the treatment of cramps in ALS/MND could be demonstrated, but many studies were underpowered to draw a definite conclusion.

S55: No study was identified using physical therapy as a therapeutic intervention for cramps.
S56: Authors' conclusions There is no evidence to support the use of any intervention for muscle cramps in ALS/MND.
S57: More and larger randomised controlled trials evaluating treatments for muscle cramps in ALS/MND are needed.
S58: P L A I N L A N G U A G E S U M M A R Y Treatment for cramps in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis/motor neuron disease A cramp is a sudden, involuntary painful contraction of a muscle.
S59: Many people with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), also known as motor neuron disease (MND), experience cramps during the course of the disease.
S60: These range from mild cramps that do not aﬀect daily activities and sleep, through to very severe, painful cramps.
S61: Some medications that are used to treat cramps in people with no medical condition or with conditions other than ALS have been tested in ALS clinical trials.
S62: These medicines include vitamin E, creatine, quinidine, and gabapentin.
S63: Other medications such as quinine sulfate, magnesium, lioresal, dantrolene, clonazepam, diphenylhydantoin, and gabapentin have been used to treat cramps in people with ALS but their eﬀectiveness is unknown.
S64: In 2006 and 2010 the US Food and Drugs Administration issued warnings concerning the use of quinine sulfate, which was the previously most widely prescribed medication for cramps in the US.
S65: This review sought to find out how eﬀective medications and physical treatments for cramps are for people with ALS.
S66: The reviewers identified 20 randomised controlled trials in people with ALS comprising a total of 4789 participants.
S67: Only one trial, of the drug tetrahydrocannabinol (THC), directly investigated the eﬀectiveness of an intervention for cramps.
S68: Thirteen randomised controlled ALS trials investigated cramps secondarily among other variables.
S69: The medications comprised vitamin E, baclofen, riluzole, L-threonine, xaliproden, indinavir, and memantine.
S70: Six randomised controlled ALS trials investigated cramps as adverse events.
S71: The medications comprised creatine, gabapentin, dextromethorphan, quinidine and lithium.
S72: None of the 20 studies could demonstrate any benefit, but the studies were small.
S73: Current evidence on the treatment of cramps in ALS is lacking and more research is needed.

S78: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews B A C K G R O U N D A cramp is a sudden, involuntary and painful muscle contraction, accompanied by palpable knotting of the muscle.
S79: The origin of cramps is incompletely understood and depends on the underlying condition (Kiernan 2001; Parisi 2000; Roeleveld 2000).
S80: Two diﬀerent pathophysiological mechanisms have been proposed to cause cramps: abnormal excitation of the terminal branches of motor axons (Bertolasi 1993; Denny-Brown 1953; Layzer 1994) and hyperexcitability or bistability of motoneurons at a spinal level (Baldissera 1994; Norris 1957; Ross 1995).
S81: Both mechanisms may complement each other (Parisi 2000).
S82: In amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), also known as motor neuron disease (MND), cramps may result from impaired inhibitory mechanisms (for example impairment of interneurons mediated by GABA, the most abundant inhibitory neurotransmitter) (Obi 1993).
S83: Many people with ALS/MND experience cramps during the course of the disease (Swash 2000).
S84: In a survey of symptom management in 84 people with ALS/MND, cramps were present in 62%, requiring treatment in 56% (Heiman-Patterson 2000).
S85: Frequently, cramps occur as the first symptom of the disease, months before the patients notice weakness and wasting (Gubbay 1985; Layzer 1982).
S86: Severity varies from mild, without aﬀecting daily activities and sleep, to disabling, where almost any voluntary muscle activity induces persistent, severely painful cramping.
S87: In ALS/MND, the cramps may aﬀect any muscle or muscle group (for example jaw, abdomen, chest, back, neck, or extremities).
S88: Various medications such as quinine sulfate, magnesium, lioresal, dantrolene, clonazepam, diphenylhydantoin and gabapentin are used for the treatment of cramps in ALS/ MND (Forshew 2003).
S89: However, treatment of cramps in ALS/ MND has been a primary endpoint only in a single randomised controlled trial with tetrahydrocannabinol (THC) (Weber 2010) and an open-label, nonrandomised study with levetiracetam (Bedlack 2009).
S90: Moreover, for quinine sulfate, the most widely prescribed medication for cramps in the US (Forshew 2003) the FDA has repeatedly issued safety announcements that it is only approved for the treatment of malaria and not approved for the treatment or prevention of night time leg cramps (FDA 2006; FDA 2010).
S91: However, oﬀ-label use, for example in therapy-refractory muscle cramps, is also discussed and prescribing physicians are advised to be alert to possible serious adverse events such as thrombocytopenia.
S92: Also, the American Academy of Neurology (AAN) practice parameter issued safety concerns about prescribing quinine for muscle cramps (Miller 2009).
S93: By contrast, a recent Cochrane review on quinine for 'idiopathic' muscle cramps found moderate quality evidence that quinine is eﬀective and moderate quality evidence of significantly increased minor adverse events (mainly gastrointestinal symptoms), but no significantly increased serious adverse events (El-Tawil 2010).
S94: In populations other than people with ALS, numerous medications, including vitamins (Chan 1998; Connolly 1992; Khajehdehi 2001), gabapentin (Mueller 1997; Serrao 2000), quinine sulfate (Connolly 1992; Gorlich 1991; Fowler 1997; Fung 1989; Gorlich 1991; Jansen 1994; Jansen 1997; Lee 1991; Man-Son-Hing 1998), L-carnitine (Ahmad 1990), prazosin (Sidhom 1994), and magnesium (Frusso 1999) have been investigated in controlled clinical trials evaluating the treatment of cramps.
S95: The conditions under investigation were haemodialysis (Ahmad 1990; Sidhom 1994), hypertension (Chan 1998), and disorders such as multiple sclerosis (Mueller 1997), cirrhosis (Lee 1991), and undefined diseases (Gorlich 1991; Fowler 1997; Serrao 2000).
S96: Other study populations included healthy people (Connolly 1992; Fung 1989; Jansen 1997; Jansen 1994; Lee 1991).
S97: A Cochrane review explored the interventions for leg cramps in pregnancy (Young 2002).
S98: Most of the studies showed a significant positive eﬀect in favour of the treatment arm.
S99: Contradictory results have been found for magnesium (positive in pregnancy (Young 2002), negative in an undefined population (Frusso 1999)) and vitamin E (positive in hemodialysis (Khajehdehi 2001), negative in veterans (Connolly 1992)).
S100: Although the pathophysiology of cramps in these disorders and ALS/MND may diﬀer (Obi 1993; Parisi 2000; Piergies 1990; Sidhom 1994), it is possible that some of the aforementioned compounds may be eﬀective in ALS/MND.
S101: Among these compounds, vitamin E (Desnuelle 2001), gabapentin (Miller 2001; Miller 1996) and dextromethorphan/quinidine (Brooks 2004) have been investigated in randomised controlled clinical trials in people with ALS.
S102: None of them, including gabapentin (Miller 2001), seemed to alleviate cramps.
S103: All published trials used either the daily number of cramps and/or a visual analogue scale (VAS) reflecting the severity of cramps.
S104: However, the VAS for cramps has never been formally validated.
S105: Nevertheless, since self-assessment of cramps was part of many controlled clinical trials in ALS, data on the eﬀect of the study medication on cramps should be available.
S106: O B J E C T I V E S To systematically assess the eﬀect of interventions on muscle cramps as a primary or secondary endpoint or adverse event in people with ALS/MND.
S107: M E T H O D S Criteria for considering studies for this review Types of studies We included only randomised and quasi-randomised trials.
S109: Types of participants We included studies with participants diagnosed with probable, probable laboratory supported, or definite ALS according to the original (Brooks 1994) or revised El Escorial criteria (Brooks 2000).
S110: Types of interventions We included studies which used oral, subcutaneous and intravenous medications or physical treatment which potentially relieve cramps in ALS/MND.
S111: Types of outcome measures Primary outcomes The primary outcome measure was the reduction in the (patient's) global impression of muscle cramp burden over a period of time rated with a visual analogue scale (VAS).
S112: A VAS is a psychometric response scale used to measure subjective characteristics or attitudes about a particular symptom.
S113: As widely used in the assessment of pain, a VAS reflects a range of no symptom burden at all (i.e.
S114: 0 or 0%) to maximal imaginable symptom burden (i.e.
S116: In this review, the muscle cramp burden reflects a summary of number, duration and intensity of muscle cramps over a period of time.
S117: This primary outcome measure was selected because it was most commonly used in the ALS studies.

S134: Search methods for identification of studies We searched the Cochrane Neuromuscular Disease Group Specialized Register (14 February 2011) using the following search terms 'amyotrophic lateral sclerosis' OR 'motor neuron disease' OR 'motor neuron disease'.
S135: We also searched the Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (CENTRAL) (Issue 1, 2011 in The Cochrane Library), MEDLINE (January 1966 to January 2011) and EMBASE (January 1980 to January 2011).
S136: We contacted pharmaceutical companies and corresponding authors of relevant unpublished or published randomised placebo controlled clinical trials in ALS/MND (included in the Cochrane Neuromuscular Disease Group Specialized Register) personally to retrieve additional data on cramps.
S137: Search strategies for MEDLINE, EMBASE and the Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (CENTRAL) are in Appendix 1; Appendix 2, and Appendix 3, respectively.
S138: Data collection and analysis All review authors screened abstracts for randomised controlled treatment trials in people with ALS/MND using the inclusion criteria stated above.
S139: If the inclusion criteria were met, we retrieved the full text article and screened it to determine whether muscle cramps were assessed.
S140: If the full text paper did not mention muscle cramps, we excluded the study.
S141: If muscle cramps were assessed as a primary or secondary outcome measure or as side eﬀects, we included the study and contacted all corresponding authors by email, and some personally.
S142: All review authors independently performed the extraction of data relating to the primary and secondary outcome measures and rated the risk of bias in the included studies.
S144: Assessment of risk of bias took into account the randomisation method, allocation concealment, participant and observer blinding, reporting of incomplete data, selective reporting, and risk of other bias.
S145: We followed the grading scale proposed in the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions 5.1.0 (Table 8.5.c: Criteria for judging risk of bias in the ‘Risk of bias’ assessment tool (Higgins 2011) and graded each criterion as fulfilled ("Low risk"), not fulfilled ("High risk") or providing insuﬀicient information to permit a "High risk" or "Low risk" judgement ("Unclear").

S157: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews We used the mean diﬀerence and 95% confidence intervals (CI) for continuous outcome measures (for example absolute change in VAS).
S158: We assessed categorical outcome measures (for example the ratio of participants experiencing cramps versus not experiencing cramps) using risk ratios (RR) and 95% CI.
S159: We planned to use a fixed- eﬀect analysis unless we found evidence of heterogeneity.
S160: R E S U L T S Description of studies The current search strategy (14 February 2011) identified 729 abstracts in MEDLINE, 796 abstracts in EMBASE, 257 abstracts in the Cochrane Neuromuscular Disease Group Specialized Register, and 455 abstracts in CENTRAL, adding up to a total of 2237 abstracts.
S161: Afer deduplication, the authors reviewed 1461 abstracts.
S162: Amongst them, we classified 1284 as not relevant to the review, leaving 177 abstracts of interest.
S163: We were unable to retrieve the full text of 13 articles (listed in Table 1).
S164: Among the 164 full text articles retrieved, the review authors identified 19 relevant articles with a total of 20 trials that met the selection criteria (Meininger 2004 comprises two studies).
S165: We excluded 158 studies (including one additional, unpublished study by Gelinas 2001 (personal communication), as well as the aforementioned 13 studies in Table 1) because they did not meet the inclusion criteria.
S166: See Characteristics of excluded studies for the full list of excluded studies.
S167: In only one instance (De Carvalho 2010) did the study authors provide original data.
S168: The 20 included trials comprised a total of 4789 participants.
S169: The studies are summarised in the Characteristics of included studies and an ongoing trial is summarised in Characteristics of ongoing studies.
S170: Thirteen studies assessed muscle cramps on a VAS once every study visit.
S171: Thus, the whole time since the last study visit was reflected in a single rating.
S172: None of the studies reported additional assessments of muscle cramps (for example by a daily or weekly log) or whether cramp number or duration of cramps were assessed.
S173: None of them specified whether the assessment by VAS reflected a global impression of cramp burden or, for instance, cramp intensity.
S174: All of them assessed muscle cramps as a secondary outcome.
S175: The review authors identified only two studies that assessed muscle cramps as a primary outcome (Weber 2010; Strong ongoing).
S176: Both assessed cramp intensity and cramp number (Strong ongoing also duration) with a daily journal covering the last 24 hours.
S177: All other studies investigated medications that aimed to modify the course of the disease or to treat emotional lability (Brooks 2004).
S178: Cramps were either assessed as secondary outcome measures, side eﬀects or as part of the study protocol.
S179: All studies except two (Brooks 2004; Chiò 2010) were RCTs comparing oral medication to placebo.
S180: Brooks 2004 compared quinidine to dextromethorphan and a combination of the two substances, Chiò 2010 compared therapeutic lithium serum levels to subtherapeutic levels.
S181: None of the studies used subcutaneous or intravenous applications and none of the studies used physical therapy as the therapeutic intervention.
S182: One study was performed over 30 years ago (Norris 1979), one over 20 years ago (Blin 1989), nine more than 10 years ago and nine within the last 10 years.
S184: Five studies were carried out in the US, 15 in western Europe.
S185: One study (Bensimon 2002) explicitly studied people with ALS aged 75 years and older, all other studies included a wide range of ages (ranging from 20 to 75 years).
S186: Study size varied considerably, ranging from very small (n = 12) to large scale (n = 959) trials.
S187: Five studies had 23 or fewer participants (Blin 1989; Blin 1992; Gil 1992; Norris 1979; Weber 2010); Scelsa 2005 had 46 participants and De Carvalho 2010 63.
S188: All other studies had 107 or more participants.
S189: Three of the five small-scale (n = 23 or less) studies used a cross-over design (Blin 1989; Gil 1992; Weber 2010), and two of them a parallel group design (Norris 1979; Blin 1992).
S190: All other studies used a parallel group design.
S191: The washout periods in the cross-over trials (Blin 1989; Gil 1992; Weber 2010) were 7, 8 and 14 days, respectively.
S192: Five studies were short term with an observational period of eight weeks or less.
S193: Fifeen studies were long term with an observational period of six months or more.
S194: Consequently, (cramp) assessment interval varied from once every week in the short-term studies to once in six months in the long-term trials.
S195: Assessment intervals are given for each study in Characteristics of included studies.
S196: Eight studies applied interventions that were used in multiple trials: three trials (Bensimon 1994; Bensimon 2002; Lacomblez 1996) studied riluzole 100 mg per day, one trial Lacomblez 1996 studied 50 mg and 200 mg riluzole per day, three trials (Blin 1989; Blin 1992; Gil 1992) used L-threonine (all 2 g per day), two trials (Meininger 2004, published as one study) investigated xaliproden.
S197: Five trials (Desnuelle 2001; Miller 2001; Norris 1979; Weber 2010; De Carvalho 2010) used medications not used in other trials (vitamin E, gabapentin, baclofen, THC, and lithium).
S198: Risk of bias in included studies The review authors graded each individual quality criterion for each study as set out in the methods section and tabulated individual scores (Characteristics of included studies).
S199: The quality of the trials varied considerably.
S200: Newer trials and trials conducted by professional pharmaceutical investigators were of better design, incorporating more appropriate statistical analysis, applying intention-to-treat analysis and taking into consideration baseline diﬀerences.
S201: Eﬀects of interventions Primary outcome measure (reduction of impression of cramp burden over time rated by VAS) The interventions comprised vitamin E, baclofen, riluzole, L- threonine, xaliproden, indinavir, memantine, and THC.
S202: All 14 studies reported statistically nonsignificant diﬀerences between the intervention and control groups.
S203: One ongoing study identified by personal communication is assessing the eﬀect of vitamin E on muscle cramps as a primary outcome measure (Strong ongoing).
S204: A meta-analysis could be performed for L-threonine (Blin 1992; Gil 1992).
S205: The combined mean change of the cramp intensity was -12.61 on a VAS (95% CI - 26.94 to 1.72) and the diﬀerence was statistically nonsignificant (P = 0.08) (see Figure 2).
S206: Data for the interventions vitamin E and THC were also reported allowing reanalysis (Desnuelle 2001; Weber 2010) (see Analysis 2.1 and Analysis 3.1, respectively).
S207: One author (De Carvalho 2010) provided unpublished data about memantine (Analysis 4.1).

S218: D I S C U S S I O N The aim of this review was to assess the evidence for the treatment of cramps in ALS/MND.
S219: So far there is a lack of interventional studies targeted at treating cramps in ALS/MND.
S220: Treatment of cramps in ALS has been the primary endpoint in only one completed (Weber 2010) and one ongoing (Strong ongoing) RCT.
S221: The former trial, investigating the drug THC, did not find any treatment benefit (see Table 2).
S222: Thus, we also included studies assessing cramps as secondary outcome measures, as side eﬀects (for example Scelsa 2005, see Table 3) or simply as part of the study protocol without specification as a primary or secondary outcome measure.
S223: Most of the identified studies were large-scale ALS/MND trials investigating disease-modifying interventions without any indication about the usefulness of the intervention for the treatment of muscle cramps in people with ALS/MND.
S224: It is therefore not surprising that all included studies that assessed muscle cramps found statistically nonsignificant diﬀerences between the intervention and control group.
S225: However, some trials were identified in this review that used medications in ALS/MND as disease-modifying treatment which have also been used in non-ALS populations for the treatment of cramps.
S226: They include vitamin E, creatine, gabapentin, dextromethorphan and quinidine.
S227: Data from the gabapentin trial and dextromethorphan/quinidine trial were not available.
S228: The analysis of single trial data suggests that vitamin E and creatine, which have shown to be eﬀective in other conditions (Khajehdehi 2001), do not aﬀect cramps in people with ALS/MND (see Table 4 and Table 5).
S229: However, these studies were not powered for treatment eﬀects on cramps so no conclusions can be drawn with respect to their eﬀectiveness.
S230: This points to another fundamental problem of cramp trials in ALS/MND, which is that no formal studies exist about the natural course (for example prevalence, duration, frequency, and intensity).
S231: One of the inherent diﬀiculties was the assessment of the symptom of interest itself, that is muscle cramps.
S232: Total muscle cramp burden can be subdivided into number, duration and intensity (or severity, the two terms were used interchangeably in this review) of muscle cramps.
S233: As muscle cramps are in most part constituted by pain, a patient has to rate this solely subjective symptom on a scale between zero and maximal symptom burden.
S234: As in pain, this was assessed with a VAS.
S235: Of the large scale, high quality ALS/MND intervention trials, all assessed muscle cramps by VAS only at every study visit.
S236: Since no indication was given that muscle cramps were assessed at a higher frequency outside the visits (for example with a daily log), the assessments probably reflect a global impression of cramp burden over the weeks to months since the previous visit, which implements a high recall bias.
S237: To reach a more firm conclusion, future intervention trials need to assess cramp number, duration and intensity on a daily basis.
S238: Another diﬀiculty was that data on muscle cramps were not reported.
S239: Of the 20 trials assessing cramps, only five reported the data.
S240: Unpublished data could be retrieved only from one trial (De Carvalho 2010) (see Table 6).
S241: The latter and the published data were re-analysed in this review.
S242: Only two trials (Blin 1992; Gil 1992) reported on the same intervention (L-threonine) and a meta- analysis resulted in a statistically nonsignificant diﬀerence (see Table 7).
S243: In no other instance were data on cramps reported twice or more and thus we were not able to pool the data.
S244: A U T H O R S ' C O N C L U S I O N S Implications for practice Based on results of this review, there is no evidence to support the use of any intervention for muscle cramps in people with ALS/MND.
S245: Implications for research More trials aimed to investigate the treatment of cramps in ALS/ MND are needed.
S246: Studies directly addressing muscle cramps in ALS should assess number and duration of muscle cramps as well as intensity of muscle cramps on a VAS.

S1612: D I F F E R E N C E S B E T W E E N P R O T O C O L A N D R E V I E W 1.
S1613: The first search included the search terms "motor neuron disease", "cramp" and "clinical trial".
S1614: Afer updating the first search a number of relevant studies which were retrieved during the first search were no longer identified.
S1615: Afer omitting "cramp" as a search term, relevant studies from the first search were again identified but at the cost of expanding the search to all (randomised controlled) intervention trials (in ALS).
S1617: Primary outcome measure: we changed "absolute change in analogue rating scale of cramps one month afer initiation of the intervention" to "reduction in the (patient's) global impression of muscle cramp burden over a period of time rated with a visual analogue scale (VAS)" since the vast majority of studies used this global measure and did not assess number, duration and intensity (or severity) of muscle cramps.
S1618: Furthermore, no studies used a four-week intervention period.
S1620: Secondary outcome measures: because the outcomes "duration of cramps" and "number of days/nights per week during which the participants had muscle cramps (cramp days)" were not used in any of the trials, we eliminated them.
S1621: Since one study assessed the "cramp intensity in the last 24 hours" and "number of cramps in the last 24 hours", these outcome measures were included.
S1622: Since quality of life and adverse eﬀects are also important outcome measures likely to be assessed in studies primarily designed to treat cramps, we also included these.
S1624: Afer it became apparent that only few data could be retrieved from existing trials, we included studies using subcutaneous and intravenous medications (in addition to oral medication and physical therapy).
